1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
144	
145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
146	
147	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
148	
149	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
150	
151	## Always read everything
152	
153	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
154	
155	## Unstarted-phase proposals
156	
157	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
158	
159	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
160	
161	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
162	
163	The proposal answers:
164	
165	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
166	
167	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
168	
169	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
170	
171	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
172	
173	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
174	
175	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
176	
177	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
178	
179	```
180	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
181	
182	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
183	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
184	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
185	
186	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
187	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
188	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
189	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
190	...
191	
192	**`\uses` skeleton**:
193	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
194	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
195	
196	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
197	
198	**References for writer**:
199	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
200	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
201	
202	**Subphase choices exposed**:
203	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
204	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
205	```
206	
207	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
208	
209	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
210	
211	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.

21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
32	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
33	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
34	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
35	derives the genus-$0$ base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
36	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
37	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
38	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that $\mathbb P^1$ is
39	\emph{already complete} and $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
40	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
41	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut} (the project's
42	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
43	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
44	\mathbb P^1$, $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (the M\"obius scaling fixing both $0$ and
45	$\infty$), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
46	G_m$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising $f(0) = \eta_A$ and composing $h := \sigma_\times
47	\fatsemi f$, one applies additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
48	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
49	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ proper, $W = \mathbb G_m$, and
50	base points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $1 \in \mathbb G_m$. Because $0$ is a \emph{fixed point} of
51	scaling, the $W$-axis restriction $\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)$ is
52	\emph{constant} at $\eta_A$, so the decomposition collapses to $h = p \fatsemi f$, i.e.\
53	$f(\lambda x) = f(x)$. Specialising $x = 1$ gives that $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is constant, and since
54	$\mathbb G_m$ is dense in $\mathbb P^1$ with $A$ separated, $f$ is constant
55	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, via the separated-target/dominant-source handle
56	\cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}). This route uses \emph{only} the proven Corollary~1.5, a
57	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
58	$\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$, and so sidesteps the ``image of a group homomorphism is a
59	closed subgroup'' input that has no Mathlib support. The classical $\mathbb G_a$-additive
60	argument (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} via the $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility
61	\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, the heart of Milne Proposition~3.9) is retained only as the
62	Milne-faithful alternative and is \emph{off} the genus-$0$ critical path. The corollary that a
63	pointed regular map of abelian
64	varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne Corollary~1.2) is recorded
65	for completeness and for Route~A but is \emph{not} needed on the genus-$0$ path; Milne's
66	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is likewise demoted to a Route-A-only item
67	(the Albanese universal property), no longer on the genus-$0$ critical path
68	(\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). Combining these
69	gives that every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant
70	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-$0$
71	curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}, $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the
72	headline \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
73	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
74	neither Serre duality nor the FGA $\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is
75	fully decoupled from Route~A.
76	
77	\medskip
78	
79	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
80	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
81	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
82	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.

93	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
94	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
95	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a

98	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
99	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
100	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
101	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
102	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
103	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
104	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
105	\end{lemma}

113	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
114	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
115	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
116	% assertion."

148	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
149	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
150	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
151	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
152	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
153	
154	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
155	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
156	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
157	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
158	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
159	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
160	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
161	
162	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
163	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
164	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
165	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
166	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
167	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
168	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
169	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
170	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
171	  agree everywhere. \qedhere

181	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
182	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
183	  irreducible.)
184	\end{remark}

644	\section{The Milne \S I.3 chain: additivity, homomorphisms, extension}
645	
646	This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the genus-$0$ base case. The two
647	load-bearing ingredients of the \textbf{primary route} are the additive decomposition over a
648	product (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and the concrete $\mathbb
649	G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times$ on $\mathbb P^1$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}); from these
650	the base case ``every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' falls out \emph{in one shot}
651	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}): the scaling fixed point $0$ collapses one axis of the
652	decomposition, giving $f(\lambda x) = f(x)$ and hence constancy on the dense $\mathbb G_m$. The
653	classical $\mathbb G_a$-additive homomorphism step
654	(\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}) and the triviality lemma $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
655	(\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) are retained as the Milne-faithful alternative but are
656	\emph{not} on the genus-$0$ critical path. None of this uses the theorem of the cube, and --- crucially
657	--- none of it uses Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (the rational-map extension): because $f
658	\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already} a total morphism out of the complete $\mathbb
659	P^1$, there is no defect map to extend over a complete surface (see
660	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}) and
661	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) are retained in this chapter only as
662	Route-A inputs (the Albanese universal property) and are explicitly \emph{off} the genus-$0$
663	path. The genus-$0$ output is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9, ``every rational
664	(here: regular) map $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant''.

936	  % -- is standard (the Borel subgroup of $\mathrm{PGL}_2$ acting on $\mathbb P^1$).
937	  % NOTE (iter-164): the load-bearing action for the PRIMARY genus-0 route is now the MULTIPLICATIVE

941	  \textbf{The scaling action (primary).} The \emph{multiplicative scaling action}
942	  \[ \sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
943	     \text{``}\sigma_\times(x, \lambda) = \lambda x\text{''} \]
944	  (\lean name \texttt{gmScalingP1} [expected]) is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of
945	  $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ --- no indeterminacy, characteristic-free (M\"obius scaling
946	  fixing both $0$ and $\infty$) --- defined chartwise:
947	  \begin{itemize}
948	    \item On $\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ (target chart $x$): $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$,
949	      a polynomial map, regular everywhere.
950	    \item Near $\infty$ (target chart $u = 1/x$): the target coordinate $1/(\lambda x)$ rewrites as
951	      $u/\lambda$, regular everywhere on this chart because $\lambda \in \mathbb G_m$ is invertible.
952	  \end{itemize}
953	  The two target charts cover the image and overlap consistently, so $\sigma_\times$ is defined on
954	  the whole surface. It is a left action of the group $\mathbb G_m$ on $\mathbb P^1$:
955	  $\sigma_\times(x, 1) = x$ and $\sigma_\times(\sigma_\times(x, \lambda), \mu) = \sigma_\times(x,
956	  \lambda\mu)$. Crucially it has \emph{two fixed points}, $\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ and
957	  $\sigma_\times(\infty, \lambda) = \infty$ for all $\lambda$; the fixed point $0$ is what makes the
958	  $W$-axis of the additive decomposition collapse in the primary base-case proof
959	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}).

965	     \text{``}\sigma(x, y) = x + y\text{''} \]
966	  (\lean name \texttt{gaTranslationP1} [expected]), likewise a \emph{total} scheme morphism on all
967	  of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a$, defined chartwise: on $\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_a$
968	  (chart $x$) it is the polynomial map $(x, y) \mapsto x + y$; near $\infty$ ($u = 1/x$) the
969	  coordinate $1/(x + y)$ rewrites as $u/(1 + u y)$, regular at $u = 0$ (value $0$ there --- $\sigma$
970	  fixes $\infty$ for every $y$), while the locus $x + y = 0$ is covered by the chart $x$ on which it
971	  is the finite value $0$. It is a left action of $\mathbb G_a$ on $\mathbb P^1$ fixing $\infty$:
972	  $\sigma(x, 0) = x$ and $\sigma(\sigma(x, y), y') = \sigma(x, y + y')$. This companion underlies
973	  only the alternative proof of \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} and the demoted $\mathbb
974	  G_a$-additive close of the base case (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}); it is \emph{not} on the
975	  genus-$0$ critical path.

980	  \label{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
981	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}

983	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 25--26)

991	  \textbf{Off the genus-$0$ critical path (iter-164).} This $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
992	  lemma is \emph{superseded by the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut}
993	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}), which closes the
994	  base case without it. It is retained only as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9 ($\mathbb

996	  \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}; no block on the genus-$0$ critical path depends on it.

998	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let
999	  $\varphi \colon \mathbb G_a \to A$ be a homomorphism of group objects (so $\varphi(0) = 0_A$ and
1000	  $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$). Then $\varphi$ is the trivial homomorphism: it
1001	  factors through the terminal object as the constant morphism at the identity,
1002	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1003	
1004	  \medskip
1005	  \noindent\textbf{Equivalent completeness phrasing.} Equivalently: a homomorphism from an
1006	  \emph{affine connected} group object into a \emph{complete} group object is trivial. That is the
1007	  abstract content; the proof below realises it characteristic-free.
1008	\end{lemma}

1010	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1011	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1012	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1013	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1014	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1015	\begin{proof}
1016	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
1017	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1018	  Write $A$ additively (an abelian variety is commutative). We give the conceptual
1019	  \emph{completeness} proof as the abstract argument, then record Milne's elementary $\mathbb
1020	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility, which applies whenever $\varphi$ extends to a morphism on
1021	  $\mathbb P^1$ (as it does in the genus-$0$ application).
1022	
1023	  \textbf{Completeness proof.} The image $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is a connected subgroup of $A$
1024	  (image of the connected $\mathbb G_a$ under a homomorphism). As the image of a homomorphism of
1025	  algebraic groups it is a \emph{closed} subgroup of $A$, hence \emph{complete} (closed in the
1026	  complete $A$); as a homomorphic image of the \emph{affine} $\mathbb G_a$ it is moreover
1027	  \emph{affine}. A connected variety that is simultaneously complete and affine over $\bar k$ is a
1028	  single point: its ring of global regular functions is a field finite over $\bar k$, hence equal
1029	  to $\bar k$ (the $\Gamma = \bar k$ collapse already packaged for proper integral sources in
1030	  \cref{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}). Therefore $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is one point;
1031	  being a homomorphism, $\varphi$ sends $0$ there, so the point is $0_A$ and
1032	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1033	
1034	  \medskip
1035	  \noindent\textbf{Milne's elementary $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility (when $\varphi$
1036	  extends to $\mathbb P^1$).} Suppose $\varphi = f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ for a morphism
1037	  $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ (the genus-$0$ situation). Set $g := f - f(1)$, so $g(1) = 0_A$, and
1038	  form $h_\times := \sigma_\times \fatsemi g \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A$ with the
1039	  scaling companion $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Applying the additive
1040	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (complete),
1041	  $W = \mathbb G_m$, base points $1 \in \mathbb P^1$ and $1 \in \mathbb G_m$ (so
1042	  $h_\times(1, 1) = g(1) = 0_A$), the axis restrictions are $x \mapsto g(x)$ and
1043	  $\lambda \mapsto g(\lambda)$, whence $g(\lambda x) = g(x) + g(\lambda)$ on $\bar k^\times$;
1044	  re-expanding $g$ gives the multiplicative relation
1045	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$ with $c = -\varphi(1)$. Now $\varphi$ is at once
1046	  additive, $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$, and ``multiplicative-additive'',
1047	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$. Computing $\varphi((x + x')\,y)$ in two ways for
1048	  $x, x' \in \bar k$, $y \in \bar k^\times$,
1049	  \[ \varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(xy) + \varphi(x'y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') + 2\varphi(y) + 2c, \]
1050	  while also $\varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(x+x') + \varphi(y) + c = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') +
1051	  \varphi(y) + c$; equating gives $\varphi(y) = -c = \varphi(1)$ for every $y \in \bar k^\times$.
1052	  Hence $\varphi$ is constant on the dense $\mathbb G_m \subset \mathbb G_a$, so constant on
1053	  $\mathbb G_a$ (density, $A$ separated), with value $\varphi(0) = 0_A$. This is Milne's argument
1054	  verbatim and uses only the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} plus the scaling action,
1055	  avoiding the ``image is a closed subgroup'' input of the completeness proof. \qedhere
1056	\end{proof}

1164	  (the $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ lemma, the content of Milne's $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$
1165	  incompatibility), $f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ is the constant morphism at $0_A$. Hence $f$ is constant on
1166	  the dense open $\mathbb G_a \subset \mathbb P^1$; since $\mathbb G_a$ is dense and $A$ is
1167	  separated, $f$ is constant on all of $\mathbb P^1$, with value $f(0) = 0_A$ (i.e.\ the original
1168	  base value before the normalising translation). \qedhere
1169	
1170	  \medskip
1171	  \noindent\emph{Note: this route is the demoted alternative.} The primary base-case proof is the
1172	  $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut now installed directly as the proof of
1173	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the multiplicative
1174	  scaling action $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} gives $\sigma_\times \fatsemi f =
1175	  \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f$ (the $W$-axis collapses because $0$ is a scaling fixed point), so
1176	  $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is constant and $f$ is constant by density --- bypassing both the $\mathbb
1177	  G_a$-additivity step and \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, and avoiding the ``image is a closed
1178	  subgroup'' Mathlib gap. The present $\mathbb G_a$-additive lemma is retained only for fidelity to
1179	  Milne; it is not on the genus-$0$ critical path.
1180	\end{proof}

1182	\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}
1183	
1184	\begin{proposition}
1185	
1186	\leanok
1187	[A morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant]
1188	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
1189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
1190	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
1191	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single $\mathbb P^1$ factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
1192	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
1193	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
1194	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
1195	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
1196	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
1197	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an
1198	  % arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
1199	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
1200	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
1201	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
1202	  single $\bar k$-point.
1203	\end{proposition}
1204	
1205	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By assumption
1206	% there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense image, and the composite
1207	% of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
1208	% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
1209	% regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
1210	% \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
1211	\begin{proof}
1212	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
1213	  \leanok
1214	  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
1215	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We give the project's \textbf{primary} proof, the
1216	  \emph{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut}: it proves constancy \emph{in one shot} from the proven
1217	  additive decomposition (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and a scaling fixed point,
1218	  without the intermediate ``$f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ additive'' step and \emph{without} the
1219	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ triviality lemma. The theorem of the cube is \emph{not} used
1220	  (\cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}), and the map $f$ is already a morphism out of the complete

1220	  (\cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}), and the map $f$ is already a morphism out of the complete
1221	  nonsingular $\mathbb P^1$, so Milne's Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is
1222	  \emph{not} invoked either.
1223	
1224	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Choose the scaling fixed point $0 \in \mathbb G_a \subset
1225	  \mathbb P^1$ (one of the two fixed points of $\sigma_\times$). Translating in the group $A$ by
1226	  $-f(0)$, we may assume $f(0) = 0_A$; un-translating at the end restores the original value.
1227	
1228	  \textbf{The scaling shortcut.} Form the \emph{total} morphism
1229	  \[ h := \sigma_\times \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A, \qquad
1230	     \text{``}h(x, \lambda) = f(\lambda x)\text{''}, \]
1231	  using the multiplicative scaling action $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} (a genuine
1232	  total morphism --- no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
1233	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
1234	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (proper), $W = \mathbb G_m$, and base
1235	  points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $1 \in \mathbb G_m$; here $h(0, 1) = f(1 \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$, as
1236	  required. The decomposition writes $h = (p \fatsemi f_0) + (q \fatsemi g)$ with $f_0$ the
1237	  $V$-axis restriction $x \mapsto h(x, 1) = f(1 \cdot x) = f(x)$ (so $f_0 = f$) and $g$ the
1238	  $W$-axis restriction $\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$.
1239	
1240	  \textbf{The $W$-axis collapses (the load-bearing scaling fixed point).} Because $0$ is a
1241	  \emph{fixed point} of scaling, $\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ for all $\lambda$, so the $W$-axis
1242	  restriction $g$ is the \emph{constant} morphism at $0_A$ --- the identity of the hom-group
1243	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_m, A)$. The corresponding summand $q \fatsemi g$ is therefore the group
1244	  identity, and the decomposition collapses to
1245	  \[ h = p \fatsemi f, \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
1246	     \sigma_\times \fatsemi f = \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f, \qquad
1247	     \text{``}f(\lambda x) = f(x)\text{''} \quad (x \in \mathbb P^1,\ \lambda \in \mathbb G_m). \]
1248	
1249	  \textbf{Constancy via density.} Specialise $x = 1$ (precompose with $\lambda \mapsto (1,
1250	  \lambda)$): $f(\lambda) = f(1)$ for all $\lambda \in \mathbb G_m$, i.e.\ $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is
1251	  the constant morphism at $f(1)$. Since $\mathbb G_m$ is dense in the irreducible $\mathbb P^1$
1252	  and $A$ is separated, the two morphisms $f$ and $\mathrm{const}_{f(1)}$, agreeing on the
1253	  non-empty open $\mathbb G_m$, agree everywhere by the dominant-source/separated-target rigidity
1254	  handle \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (the same \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant} family
1255	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} uses). Hence $f$ is constant, with value $f(1)$; in the normalised
1256	  picture $f(0) = 0_A$, so the constant is $0_A$ (the original $f(0)$ before translation).
1257	
1258	  \medskip
1259	  \noindent\emph{Multi-factor remark.} For the general unirational statement of Proposition~3.10,
1260	  one writes $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition
1261	  (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and applies this base case to each $\beta_i \colon
1262	  \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here. The Milne-faithful $\mathbb
1263	  G_a$-additive route (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}, via $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$,
1264	  \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) reaches the same conclusion and is retained as an alternative,
1265	  but is \emph{not} on this critical path.
1266	\end{proof}

1268	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
1269	
1270	\begin{proposition}
1271	
1272	\leanok
1273	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
1274	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1275	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1276	  \uses{def:genus}
1277	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
1278	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
1279	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
1280	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
1281	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
1282	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
1283	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
1284	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
1285	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
1286	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
1287	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
1288	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1289	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1290	\end{proposition}
1291	
1292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
1293	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
1294	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
1295	\begin{proof}
1296	  \uses{def:genus}
1297	  \leanok
1298	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
1299	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
1300	
1301	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
1302	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
1303	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
1304	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
1305	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
1306	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
1307	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
1308	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
1309	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
1310	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
1311	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1312	\end{proof}
1313	
1314	\begin{remark}
1315	  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}
1316	  \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} is Riemann--Roch--flavoured and its formalisation is a
1317	  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
1318	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
1319	  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
1320	  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
1321	  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
1322	  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
1323	  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
1324	\end{remark}

1326	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-$0$ curve}
1327	
1328	\begin{theorem}
1329	
1330	\leanok
1331	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
1332	  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
1333	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
1334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
1335	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1336	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
1337	  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1338	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
1339	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
1340	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
1341	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
1342	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1343	
1344	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
1345	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
1346	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
1347	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
1348	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
1349	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
1350	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
1351	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
1352	  \eta[A]$.
1353	\end{theorem}
1354	
1355	\begin{proof}
1356	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1357	  \leanok
1358	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
1359	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
1360	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
1361	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
1362	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
1363	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
1364	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
1365	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
1366	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
1367	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1368	
1369	  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
1370	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
1371	  Once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis carried by
1372	  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
1373	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.
1374	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of $\Jac(C)$. It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let $k$ be a field. Let $X$ be a reduced geometrically irreducible $k$-scheme and let $Y$ be a separated $k$-scheme. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y$ be two morphisms of $k$-schemes, and let $U \subseteq X$ be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where $U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X$ is the canonical open immersion), then $g_1 = g_2$ as morphisms of $k$-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where $Y$ is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$ formed from the group law on $Y$. The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side $[\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$ to $[\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$.
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{$Y \to \Spec k$ is separated.} Hypothesis $[\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$ provides this directly (no projection from $\mathtt{IsProper}$ needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form $\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)$ that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{$X$ is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis $[\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]$ over the field-spectrum base $\Spec k$ (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields $\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}$ via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion $U.\iota$ is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis $[\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]$, the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality $g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}$ from the agreement-on-$U$ hypothesis. The promotion to $g_1 = g_2$ in $\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)$ uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of $X.\mathrm{left}$.
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion $U.\iota$ from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to $\mathrm{Over}$-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on $X$ and $Y$ are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-$\bar k$ argument (for $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ hitting the identity at a $\bar k$-point) to this theorem with $X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ and $Y := A_{\bar k}$. The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property of $\Jac(C)$): given two morphisms $g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A$ with $g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P$, scheme-level agreement on the image of $\alpha_P$ (a non-empty open after passing to $C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)$) forces $g_1 = g_2$.
59	\end{itemize}
